title: Job 34
subtitle: Elihu’s Second Speech: God Cannot Do Wrong

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — “It Is Unthinkable That God Would Do Wrong”

heb.paragraph: "Far be it (ḥālîlâ) from God to do evil, from the Almighty to do wrong" (v.10). Elihu defends God’s justice categorically. He quotes Job (v.5: "I am innocent but God denies me justice") and responds: it is unthinkable that God perverts justice. This is the friends’ position restated, but with stronger philosophical grounding.

refs.note: "Will not the Judge of all the earth do right?" — Abraham’s foundational question, which Elihu answers affirmatively.
"Is God unjust? Not at all!" — Paul’s answer to the same question.

notes.note: Elihu quotes Job: "I am in the right, but God denies me justice." He engages Job’s actual words, which is progress beyond the friends.
"Far be it from God to do evil! He repays everyone for what they have done. It is unthinkable that God would do wrong." Elihu affirms divine justice as a necessary truth.
"If it were his intention and he withdrew his spirit and breath, all humanity would perish together." The argument from sustaining power: God who sustains all life cannot be life’s enemy.

Calvin: "far be it from God to do evil" is theologically correct. But Elihu, like the friends, struggles to reconcile this truth with Job’s experience. The assertion is right; the application to Job’s case is incomplete.
Calvin: the sustaining-power argument is Elihu’s best. God’s ongoing maintenance of creation is continuous mercy. Even in suffering, the fact that we exist at all is evidence of divine restraint.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ḥālîlâ is an emphatic denial: "profanity!" or "God forbid!" It expresses horror at the very idea. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

The NET: the withdrawal of God’s spirit (rûaḥ) is the reverse of Genesis 2:7. God breathed life in; if he breathed it back, life ends. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: Elihu’s argument is philosophical rather than experiential. God MUST be just because the alternative is incoherent. This is stronger than the friends’ appeal to observation but still does not address Job’s experience.

Clines: the sustaining-power argument is genuinely interesting. If God were malicious, he could simply withdraw his breath and everything dies. The fact that life continues proves God is not hostile. But it does not prove God is fair in every case.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Elihu argues from divine necessity. God cannot do wrong because God’s nature is justice. The argument is metaphysical, not empirical — a different approach from the friends. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Elihu addresses the assembled listeners (vv.2–4) and quotes Job directly (v.5–6). His response is essentially: God IS just because God MUST be just. If God were unjust, creation itself would collapse (vv.14–15). The argument from necessity: if God withdrew his spirit, all life would perish. God sustains everything; therefore God cannot be malicious.

heb.paragraph: "Can someone who hates justice (mishpāṭ) govern?" (v.17). Elihu’s rhetorical question: if God governs, he must love justice, because governance requires justice. The one who holds the universe together cannot be the one who tears justice apart.

refs.note: "From heaven the LORD looks down and sees all mankind" — the omniscience Elihu invokes.
"He changes times and seasons; he deposes kings and raises up others" — the sovereign governance Elihu describes.

notes.note: "Can someone who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the just and mighty One? Is he not the One who says to kings, ‘You are worthless’?" Elihu argues from political analogy: the sovereign must love justice.
"His eyes are on the ways of mortals; he sees their every step. There is no deep shadow, no utter darkness, where evildoers can hide." God’s omniscience means no injustice goes unnoticed.
"Suppose someone says to God, ‘I am guilty but will offend no more.’ Should God then reward you on your terms?" Elihu challenges Job’s demand for a specific kind of justice.
"Job speaks without knowledge; his words lack insight." Elihu’s verdict echoes what God will say from the whirlwind (38:2). This is either prophetic anticipation or the source God borrows from.

Calvin: "can someone who hates justice govern?" is a strong argument. Governance requires justice. But it is an argument about God’s character, not about Job’s specific case. Elihu proves God must be just without explaining how justice operates in this instance.
Calvin: the accusation of "rebellion" goes too far. Job’s protest was bold but not rebellious. God himself will vindicate Job as one who "spoke what is right" (42:7).

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the logic is: governance requires justice; God governs; therefore God is just. The syllogism is valid but does not address exceptions.
The NET: "adds rebellion to his sin" — the word for rebellion (peshaʿ) implies willful defiance against a sovereign. Elihu treats Job’s complaint as political insurrection.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: "he adds rebellion to his sin" is Elihu at his harshest. He accuses Job of compounding the problem by protesting. But Clines notes: God never endorses this accusation. In the epilogue, God rebukes the friends, not Job.

Clines: "Job speaks without knowledge" anticipates God’s opening words in 38:2. Whether Elihu is prophetically anticipating God or merely saying the same thing for different reasons is the central question of the Elihu speeches.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Elihu’s second speech moves from philosophical defence of divine justice to accusation of Job. The shift mirrors the friends’ trajectory: start with theology, end with condemnation. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Elihu presses his case: God is impartial (v.19), sees all (v.21–22), removes leaders without investigation because he already knows (v.24–25), overthrows the wicked in the sight of all (v.26). His conclusion: Job adds rebellion to his sin by multiplying words against God (v.37). Elihu is harsher here than in his first speech, moving from pedagogy toward accusation.

ppl.name: Job
ppl.text: Elihu quotes Job: "I am in the right, but God denies me justice…

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: His response is essentially: God IS just because God MUST be just…

rec.text: "Elihu quotes Job: "I am in the right, but God denies me justice." He engages Job’s actual words, which is progress beyond the friends."

rows.label: The Accusation — 34:16–37
rows.text: "Job speaks without knowledge." Elihu moves from defence of God to accusation of Job.

rows.label: The Accusation — 34:16–37
rows.text: “Job speaks without knowledge.” Elihu moves from God’s defence to Job’s accusation.

hebtext.note: "Far be it (ḥālîlâ) from God to do evil, from the Almighty to do wrong" (v.10). Elihu defends God’s justice categorically. He quotes Job (v.5: "I am innocent but God denies me justice") and responds: it is unthinkable that God perverts justice. This is the friends’ position restated, but with stronger philosophical grounding.

"Can someone who hates justice (mishpāṭ) govern?" (v.17). Elihu’s rhetorical question: if God governs, he must love justice, because governance requires justice. The one who holds the universe together cannot be the one who tears justice apart.

thread.text: "Will not the Judge of all the earth do right?" — Abraham’s foundational question, which Elihu answers affirmatively.
"Is God unjust? Not at all!" — Paul’s answer to the same question.
"From heaven the LORD looks down and sees all mankind" — the omniscience Elihu invokes.
"He changes times and seasons; he deposes kings and raises up others" — the sovereign governance Elihu describes.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Justice both high: Elihu defends divine governance. Prophecy: 'speaks without knowledge' anticipates God’s whirlwind words (38:2).

prayer_prompt: As you consider Elihu's defense of God's justice — 'It is unthinkable that God would do wrong' — consider asking God whether defending His character requires you to deny the complexity of human experience, or whether both can be held together.

coaching.tip: Elihu defends God's justice: 'It is unthinkable that God would do wrong' (v. 12). His argument resembles the friends' but with different emphasis. He's not accusing Job of sin but defending God's character against Job's accusations. The question isn't 'What did Job do?' but 'How can God be unjust?' Elihu thinks Job has overreached in his complaints.

'Should God then reward you on your terms?' (v. 33). Elihu identifies Job's demand for vindication as potentially problematic — Job wants God to conform to human standards of justice. But what if divine justice operates on a larger scale? Elihu isn't wrong that Job's expectations may need recalibrating. God's speeches will confirm this, but differently than Elihu imagines.
